Title: One Day in America
URL: https://www.esquire.com/news-politics/a71427015/one-day-in-america/
Time Published: 2026-06-23T10:13:24Z
Full Content:
Thirty-eight glimpses of a day 250 years in the making. So what is America? Do you know? Do any of us know anymore? We’re hermetically sealed in our information bubbles, sorted by our social-media feeds, feverishly working alone in our home offices, picking up essentials with a swipe and a tap rather than a trip to the store. You’ve heard a million times that we’re polarized, but this is something else: We’re atomized. Absent from one another. Us, here in this place that puts a lot of stock in a very important idea about collectivism: “Out of many, one.” (Perhaps you know the Latin: E pluribus unum.) If you haven’t heard, America’s got kind of a big birthday coming up. The lady turns 250 this year, and that means it’s high time to consider how she’s doing. To consider what she is. To consider who we are. It’s a tall order, trying to get your arms around a country the size of America, and maybe that’s why it’s been so easy for so many of us to slip into an existence in which we’ve lost touch with one another. Luckily, there’s a very simple way to halt the slide. It’s kind of the whole point of democracy, but we often fail to heed it outside of the formality of voting: Our civic duty is to listen to one another. If you want to know what America is, well—listen to Americans. Not so easy, of course; there are almost 350 million of us. And we’re pretty much all loud. But Esquire decided to get as close as a magazine can. On a single Friday in April, we carried pen and camera from sea to shining sea, visiting with Americans as far apart as a Hollywood scion and the diner waitress in the small town in Maine where the sun first hits the country. We covered thirty-three cities, both states outside the lower forty-eight, D.C., and Puerto Rico. We went fishing, we went farming, we flew, we partied, we ate Italian beef. So what is America? Many things. But it’s 2026, so let’s start here: It’s a gamble. I’m at a blackjack table when the woman next to me says she sees a bird. She’s forty or so, wearing a nice caftan and running shoes. She’s got her eyes up, hitching a golden shoulder bag across her chest, looking for the bird again. According to her, it’s careening among the crackling LED light of the slot machines surrounding us. “It’s called a phoebe,” she says, squinting. The dealer, trained to keep his eyes on the table, won’t look for the bird. “Birds happen,” he says. And me, I just can’t see it: bad eyes, too much hubbub, six hours of 3-6 Hold-’em with a half-kill, forty-three minutes of surprisingly lucky roulette, one bowl of crab soup, and three bets in the sportsbook. I was up eleven hundred bucks, which for me is what I term a nice circumstance there. And then blackjack at what turns out to be a pretty hot table. So yeah, we’re rolling near midnight in a desert casino outside Palm Springs, at the bottom of southern California. With a bird. Again and again, the woman gently drops two green chips onto the felt. Fifty bucks a hand. She’s got black chips to spare. “I’m a bird-watcher,” she says. “For serious, that’s a black phoebe.” What do I know? But the dealer has five. She doubles down on a nine and wins. I split my eights and win twice. It’s all good. But before another card falls, three frat-tastic college knobs jam up to the table, breathing all over the whole setup. Goodbye, hot table. The smallest one, with his Red Sox hat on backward, asks the dealer if they can bet seventy-eight dollars on a hand. It’s the last of their chips, he tells the world, and they want one last stab. Imagine. We’re in the middle of a shoe here, and I want to protest. But I notice another of the knobs has car keys in his hand, and all of a sudden I know what time it is. Yeah, yeah. No clocks in a casino. Everybody knows. But I can always sense the end of day from my seat at a gaming table. It comes to me in small signals: A guy glances at his watch while losing at roulette. A wandering girl lays her head on the shoulder of a bestie as they pass. Bearded baseball-hat guys step away from a losing table, shooting a glance back at the dealer whisking away their last palmful of chips. And guys like these guys, clenching car keys while betting one more time. So midnight has come, and everything in the once jolly churn of the casino looks a little kite-strung and stressed by the entirety of the day prior. In this way, the brokedown palace of dreams makes itself known to me once more. I no longer like the vibe. I pick up my bet, tip the dealer, and say bye to the woman. It’s over. Nothing lasts in a casino, but everything keeps going all the same. I cash out with $1,300, a nice circumstance there. Yeah, I’ll be back. Why not? It’s a new day. —Tom Chiarella Smalls is a basement club, but it feels more like a catacomb. Because cat-a-comb is syncopated, and because it is decorated with images of saints like Louis Armstrong and Charlie Parker, and because it is consecrated by the acts of the people who come to play on nights like this. The ones just now filing down the staircase from the street, carrying their gig bags to the front, extracting their horns, checking their strings, stretching their fingers. They’re here for the night’s jam session. You’re here for something else. You’re trying to understand why jazz still matters. Why this music you scarcely understand, decades past its heyday, can still beckon you out, irresponsibly late, on a weeknight. A band assembles. At the front of the stage, a saxophone and two trumpets count in the first song. They’re an unlikely trio—one in a slim black suit, another in a tee that just says “snowflake,” the third, honestly, kind of a doofus—but they blow a breakneck motif in stunning synchrony. Then comes the holy mystery: They trade off dizzying runs, one at a time, madcap drum fills in between, timing perfect with nary a word or glance between them. “Yes sir!” exclaims an older Black man in a porkpie hat, wedged in the crook where the wall meets the bar. “Yes sir! Yes sir!” Smalls is the last club in the city committed to the jam. Every day of the week a musician can come and make music with complete strangers, slipping on and off the stage as he feels called. Jams go into the wee hours, full to the matins. Another song. The new bassist wears a tee from the Slutty Vegan. The drummer is a young woman with purple hair and pink-and-purple slides on her feet. She races from floor tom to snare to hi-hat to crash, mouth open, hair flying everywhere. When the song ends, she walks matter-of-factly to the back of the club, up the stairs, out to the street. “That was amazing,” someone gasps from the bar. It’s late enough now that every new song is a dare, even for the night-owl jazz cats. “We need a bassist.” “Bro don’t look at me!” “Where to next?” “I’m trying to go home.” Someone always, inevitably, sits in. A pianist moves over to the bass. The trombonist from the start of the night returns. A horn player strolls up from the depths, playing as they walk. When the crowd finally thins and the bartender is polishing the night’s last glasses, the man in the porkpie hat, sans hat, strides to the microphone. “Lady Bird,” he calls out, and a bassist plucks out a bass line. A young Asian woman is on the keys. We fit together like two birds of a feather, the man sings, as the woman pours herself into a delicate melody. The perfect combination and it couldn’t be better. It’s so beautiful that it brings to mind a memory: A man you knew, new to the States, was asked what he made of the country. “Well, when they dig up America in a thousand years,” he said, “I guess there will be three things that are left: jazz, baseball, and the Gettysburg Address.” Yes sir! This mysterious language, this dialect of chaotic improvisation and unexpected unison and swing? More American than English. And that is why it matters. Yes sir! Yes sir! The bassist retires, but the pianist keeps playing and the man keeps singing. If you just say the word I’ll leave my lonely world / I’ll fly with you Lady Bird. You make your way out to the street. Musicians are standing around, talking, figuring ways home. One asks to bum a cigarette. You don’t smoke. He shrugs and wanders off. It’s 3:26 a.m. Smalls is closing down; porters are bringing garbage bags out to the street. Every time the door swings open, you can still hear the woman playing the piano. —Kevin Dupzyk I’ve lost about forty-three pounds, roughly twenty kilos, since July. No operations. It’s just been diet and exercise. Today I woke up at 4:00 a.m. and I was at the gym around 4:30. It’s early, but I’ve always been an early riser and I take coffee before I leave. I do that four days a week. Two days are leg days, and two days are upper-body days. Once or twice a week, when her schedule allows, my daughter comes with me. She actually has the certifications to be a personal trainer, so she’s my personal trainer. There comes an age, though, at which children don’t really want to spend that much time with their parents, so she just tells me what to do and goes to do her own exercise. I still like having her there. But she works and she is a student, so today she wasn’t able to come. I did my exercises under my own direction: the dead lift, squats, and leg press. My routine takes about an hour, so I was in the sauna a little after 5:30 and back in my car at 6:30 or maybe 6:45. When I got home, my wife and daughter were getting ready to leave, for work and for college. I said goodbye to them, and they got in their cars. Then they called each other and stayed on the line until they reached their destinations. They did this in case one of them got stopped by Border Patrol or ICE. My family came to the United States from Venezuela and claimed asylum eleven years ago. We’ve been waiting all this time simply to have an interview scheduled to determine if we can stay. In the meantime, thanks to work permits, we’ve been able to build a life here. Until recently, I did not feel afraid—but now even people with work permits are sometimes being detained. We have seen less ICE activity near where we live lately, but we live in anxiety, so for now this is our plan. Constant communication. That’s the other reason I go to my gym so early. What we’ve seen is that Border Patrol and ICE are patrolling closer to about 7:00 a.m. Still, when I go, I have a binder in my car with copies of all of my important papers, all my immigration paperwork and my work permit, that prove that I am here lawfully. My wife and my daughter do the same, but they still have to leave when there is a higher chance of an encounter. Most of life can’t be accomplished before 7:00 a.m. Not like leg day. —Adel, an asylum seeker from Venezuela, as told to Kevin Dupzyk “In the old days, there was only local flowers,” says Vincent, fourth-generation florist and owner of the Wall Street Florals stall at the Original Los Angeles Flower Market, on the same spot where his grandmother worked in the 1920s. “We were the middleman between the growers and the florists.” The market opened in 1919 as a wholesaler. Today importing is easier, and there’s Amazon. “There’s less of a niche for us now,” he says. Still, even as it is pitch-dark outside, the three-dozen-ish stalls in the massive warehouse are filling with color, petal by petal. Workers process deliveries, rotate stock, and arrange bouquets. The market has flowers from all over: Ecuador, Colombia, Japan. In half an hour, as they have for a century, buyers from L.A.’s floral shops will come scoop up their orders. But maybe not as many as last year. “Flowers are a luxury,” says Diego, a cheery salesman for the Flores Deos stall. “When the economy wanes, that’s one of the first things that gets cut.” Easter and Christmas used to be busy; Valentine’s and Mother’s Day have held on. “Prom season won’t hit for another few weeks,” Vincent’s niece Qiana adds. “We’re not doing boutonnieres and corsages yet.” “You know what’s big now,” Vincent says, “is graduation. It used to be high school and college.” He laughs. “Now the dance class for your three-year-old has a graduation.” The economy isn’t the whole story, though. “A few crazy things are happening,” a man named Manuel tells me as he pushes a pallet of money trees out onto the sales floor. “The ICE raids shut us down,” Vincent explains. “Legal and illegal, people just stayed away.” The raids are still happening. “Where was the last one,” Vincent asks Qiana, “the one that happened two weeks ago?” She says, “There was one over by Santee Alley,” a nearby outdoor bazaar. “But it wasn’t on the news,” Vincent says, “so it didn’t affect us. When it’s on the news, everyone gets...” He grits his teeth and raises his eyebrows, the international symbol for fear. Vincent recalls a moment when the ICE-related tension was at its highest, when word of a nearby sweep sent people at the market running. “I don’t think they got anyone here, right?” “Nobody here,” Qiana agrees. “But this whole swarm just sprinted out,” he says. “That was at the beginning, when ICE was just rounding people up like animals.” The market feels like it’s coming back to life, though. The coffee cart is brewing the day’s first urn, a couple radios are bumping reggaeton on Mega 96.3 FM, a wagon of hydrangeas rattles by. Soon the doors open. Next to a wooden sign that says “Blessed” in cursive, Diego looks on the bright side. “Bad things happen,” he says, “but the next day you have to put on a good face and clock in.” —Dave Holmes Cadillac Mountain, in Acadia National Park on Maine’s Downeast coast, often claims to have the first sunrise in the country. For half the year, that’s true. But from late March to mid-September, the sun rises far enough to the northeast that a 1,742-foot ridge about three hours away sees light sooner. Mars Hill Mountain is in another world. Its inland valley traps cold northern air, yielding a temperature inversion that locks fog in. The sunrise here doesn’t turn the sky dark to gold; it goes dark to gray. But it’s still the first, earning its spot on the seal of the village of Mars Hill, population 1,360. It’s a quiet place. On this morning, ten regulars came through Al’s Diner, established in 1977 on Main Street, for conversations that seemed to pick up exactly where the previous day’s left off. The waitress, Corinna Willis, has lived in Mars Hill since she was nine. Everyone checks in on each other, she says. It can be slow, but life here is predictable and steady in the best way. Just like the sun coming up. —Eli Wright International Dark Sky Week, which ran this year from April 13 to April 20, either celebrates natural light or trolls artificial light, depending on how you look at it. Which one is a comet? A comet’s light is stolen, sunlight refracted through the dust and gas and particles given off by a rock hurtling through space. Smack in the middle of Dark Sky Week, astronomer Qicheng Zhang was at the Lowell Discovery Telescope, located well above seven thousand feet near a former logging camp, to observe a comet known by the unwieldy name C/2025 R3 (PanSTARRS). It was the second-brightest comet you would be able to see from the ground this year. Zhang was observing it to understand what kind of dust was being ejected, which is worth knowing because the particles can come rocketing down to earth or interfere with satellites—but also because, Zhang says, “it’s pretty.” The real beauty of such a comet, however, may lie in its ephemerality. The LDT is a facility of the famed Lowell Observatory, where Pluto, once a planet but a planet no longer, was discovered. The comet C/2025 R3 (PanSTARRS) last appeared in the solar system about 180,000 years ago, Zhang estimates. After this pass, it will be ejected from the solar system and likely never be seen by earthlings again. Not in our lifetimes. Not in America’s lifetime. Not ever. It exists for us in this moment alone. —Kevin Dupzyk Isn’t it a beautiful day here at the old Dueling Grounds! A bright morning in early spring, the sun just up, the birds singing, the world awakening. A few people are about—walking dogs, getting in a morning run or workout, taking in the majestic view of Manhattan across the Hudson. Look around, look around—how lucky we are to be alive right now indeed. The whole scene is just beaming, and you’d be hard-pressed to guess the bloody-minded affair that went down here a little more than 200 years ago on just such a daybreak as this. In a clearing on a ledge somewhere just there below, Alexander Hamilton—hero of the Revolution and first treasury secretary, architect of the fledgling economy—and Aaron Burr, the sitting vice president, after years of political and personal animosity, squared off at twenty paces with pistols. The duel left Hamilton dead and Burr a pariah the rest of his days. It underscored the inexorable rise of political parties, and partisan divide, at the start of the republic. What better place to contemplate our current difficulties than the site of one of our seminal acts of political violence? There’s little here now to mark the occasion. A tiny fenced-in memorial features a bust of Hamilton on a small pedestal, the rock where he supposedly laid his head after he was shot, and a beat-up plaque commemorating those who “came to Weehawken to defend their honor, according to the custom of the day.” It seems to function not so much as attraction or destination but rather as backdrop, wallpaper to daily life. Half a block north, there’s a crescent of brilliant green—commemorative Hamilton Park—sliced out of the very edge of the Palisades. Are the New Jerseyans here on this fine morning aware of the events the site commemorates? An impromptu informal survey among these morning people turns up only a vague notion: something from the early days, yes. A military skirmish maybe? offers one guy, elbows on the park bench behind him, relaxing into his morning. Or wasn’t it a fiery speech some president or senator gave from this spot? says another guy, squinting into the distance. And yet even as what happened here has faded into obscurity—cue the tut-tutting about how we Americans have no sense of history—the kind of corrosive grudge that gave rise to dueling feels as present and inevitable in the country right now as the steady flow of the Hudson into New York Harbor. Josephine and Mita, a couple kitted out in workout gear, are getting in a morning routine on a swath of park grass, doing yoga-like stretches. What brings them here, and what do they make of the state of things? “Here you get a little nature, and you get to view this beautiful city, and we just kind of center ourselves before we start work,” Josephine says. “It’s the best part of my day,” says Mita. “This is the best view I have ever seen. Look at the city—its hands up! They are saying hi to you.” Josephine grew up in Brooklyn but is originally from Belize, and Mita is from Belarus. They both live here now, in Union City, a few minutes away. “Instead of focusing on things we don’t have control over,” Josephine says, “this, coming here to start our day, we have control over. “I think what we have to remember is that we are being used,” she continues. “Divide and conquer. I’m from Belize, I’m of color. He’s from Belarus. And we are fortunate that we have friends from all over the world—any part of the world you can think of—we have friends over for dinner. We’ll have somebody from Ukraine and Moscow coming to dinner at the same table, friends. We have Jewish people with Palestinians in our home.” Down below the cliff edge, the precise spot where pistols were drawn has been lost to time—overgrown by brush, covered by railroad tracks and a brand-new row of sleek condos. And yes, the knock on Americans has always been that we have no care for our own history, that we’re so busy looking ahead, searching for a bright future, that we don’t often look back. Maybe that’s not always a bad thing. —John Kenney You have to come early to get a spot on ‘Ōhua Avenue, across the street from Queens Beach. Waikiki is lousy with vacationers. Hoku Kupihea has been a surfboard shaper for a decade or more, and usually his van is full of blanks. Today he has only one board, one he crafted just for himself, just for this wave, the wave at Queens. He carries it through the crowds of Honolulu’s famous neighborhood, the kind of place we build in lots of our cities: It caters to tourists but is still home to people like Hoku, who has lived here forever and knows this wave as an old friend. He paddles out. He surfs. The board is a beauty. —Tommy Pierucki California “We are turned the wrong way,” says the pilot. Not what I want to hear after my long flight from Hawaii to LAX lands. But the plane finds a gate, and my fiancée and I make our transfer. Only five hours home to New York. As the engines roar and our next plane leaves the ground, I stare out the window and see the city turn to ocean, ocean turn to cloud, and cloud turn to nation. The service dog in front is calm. Nevada? Arizona? It looks like Arrakis from Dune down there. Around this time, a flight attendant stops by with the snack and drink cart. I get my usual: a Coke and Biscoff cookies. Colorado The Rockies flatten. Green and brown crop circles dot an endless sea of dirt. Clouds roll in, obscuring my view. I’m half asleep as it is, so I close my eyes, stretch out, and thank God for my short legs. Illinois America looks amazing from above. Between green and brown rectangles, little metropolitan organs connect via highway arteries. Also, my ass is sore. But here’s the snack cart! I get Sun Chips, Cheez-Its, and, of course, a Coke. Ohio I notice on the in-flight map that we’re making circles above Cleveland. The arrival time keeps ticking down from one hour fifteen minutes to one hour five, then back up again. Ohio We’ve done at least three loops. Ohio That dog is going to need to piss. Ohio Finally the pilot tells us we’re in a holding pattern with JFK and that we don’t have enough fuel to keep circling. We may make an emergency stop in Detroit. Michigan We make an emergency stop in Detroit. The vibes on the plane are bad. People stand, stretch their legs, open overhead bins. The bathroom line is no joke. Michigan We take off. Again. Did I mention I already flew like six hours from Hawaii? Pennsylvania Outside, it’s green as far as I can see, interrupted only by the blue of small bodies of water. It’s more lush than where I grew up outside Fargo, North Dakota, but it makes me miss the Midwest for just a second. I close my eyes again. New Jersey I have never been so happy to see New Jersey. New York Waves roll into the east Atlantic beaches as we descend into an overcast Queens. A collective exhale is felt in the cabin. The dog has no idea. We’re greeted by, no shit, a rainbow. Air travel really is amazing. —Bryn Gelbart Beneath an early-morning sky the color of pale piss, a man takes a leak in the scrublands near an underpass. Down San Ysidro Boulevard outside the El Super grocery store, a young woman at a table asks customers in Spanish to help a citizen effort to keep local property taxes low. A half mile in the other direction, down by the train depot across from the U.S. Customs and Border Protection Port of Entry, everyone carries a load. They drag wheeled suitcases with shopping bags and duffels bungee-corded to the handles. They walk under the weight of old backpacks packed to bulging. They carry garbage bags in the crooks of their arms. Most of the storefronts are money exchanges. Casas de cambios. There’s a mini-mall galleria with a McDonald’s, a souvenir shop, and a few stores like Shoes 4 Less that sell sneakers, backpacks, underwear, umbrellas. Outside the gleaming Port of Entry—erected in 2014, Obama’s name on it— there’s a sign that reads, “To Mexico,” and you can walk up a ramp to Mexico. (And then you can turn right around and reenter the U.S. through the Port of Entry.) On the ramp, you pass under delicate arcs of stainless steel, an installation called Of a Leaf or a Feather, by the Japanese sculptor Norie Sato. According to the plaque, “Sato was inspired by the idea of protection … and sought to create an artwork that would soften and humanize the site. The individual elements take their shape from the leaves of the indigenous agave plant, as well as the feathers of the American bald eagle.” Further up the ramp as you approach the wide, prison-style turnstile that leads to Mexico, you pass under rows of razor wire coiled tight, security cameras stacked on top of one another, and what appears to be a section of President Trump’s towering border wall. A security guard in a store nearby was born in California but, he says, “I’m Mexican.” Growing up, he too used to drag a suitcase up the ramp pretending he was a tourist; it was usually full of dirty laundry he was bringing to do at his mother’s house in Tijuana. “Some people live in Mexico but low-key work here,” he says. I ask him if more ICE agents than usual have turned up around San Ysidro in recent months. “No, not really,” he says. “I wish they would. Because all the people here would band together and fuck them up.” At El Rincon, a family-run restaurant nearby, the Menudo Rojo is a rich stew of beef tripe cooked in a red chile broth deep with flavor and studded with hominy. The recipe here is from the owner, Silvana Alaniz, and they say it’s good for hangovers. Before your meal comes out, a waitress walks around with a basket of sweet pastries, baked each morning and carried across the border, which she offers as pan. I pick a sugared, doughy roll with an oozy pineapple filling. She uses tongs to set it on my plate. I take bites in between the soup to ease the spice. This is a border town. The sweet roll and the hot soup slow the place down and make it feel like a destination, when to so many people San Ysidro is a place on the way to somewhere else, or something else, something better, and you wonder about the lives in those suitcases and the people who took what they could carry and left the rest. —Ryan D’Agostino I drive to the Houston Chronicle building by muscle memory, so I forget until I’m nearly there that I need to add two lights and six minutes to my commute because the closest parking-lot gate has been out of commission since a car crashed into it during a police chase in February. A friend and I bet on when it might get fixed. I chose the Ides of March. The over has won. The fourth-floor newsroom is empty enough to make me wonder if the rapture has come and gone without me. Fridays are never crowded: We are only mandated to be in the office three days a week, and we should be out in the field more than that, but I am startled every time I’m the only person in the too-big room. I settle at my desk—relatively clean, by which I mean the half-full notebooks are piled atop a book of obituary-themed cocktails and nothing has fallen off my cubicle walls—and email the new members of the beleaguered agency that regulates funerals. That story—about the funeral commission—runs Monday; I’m chasing four others today. This, sometimes, is the chaos of life as a reporter in the country’s fourth-largest city with a metro area more populous than Massachusetts. I run to a coffee shop to meet a woman about whom I’m writing a profile. She spent most of her career in a prosecutor’s office, working on convictions of people she believed were guilty. Now she realizes several were not. She has apologized to several defendants. As a form of tangible atonement, she is running to be a judge on a statewide criminal appeals court. We sit at an uneven outdoor picnic table. I ask about her Starry Night dress. I like these questions that seem off-topic, because the answers can contain an interview’s most meaningful specifics. (This one does not, but she tells me about her photography background, so I learn something new about her.) She describes a recent campaign stop that began without any attendees and ended at a community-play rehearsal. Often, she says, she goes on the trail and talks to people about issues entirely unrelated to her campaign. She fiercely wants to win, but she has decided that she will count each connection she makes with another human as a success. I will spend more time with her, but I leave with that—the story’s heart. There are five of us by the time I return to the office just after noon. One colleague discusses housing contracts. Papers rustle as the crime and courts reporters pick through case files for a story they’re partnering on. (Him, crime: a Boston sports devotee quick with a muttered quip. Her, courts: a high-energy tabloid expat who shares updates on her reporting in a half shout that I find grounding in an office where too much of our communication takes place on Slack instead of out loud.) The cops reporter—a friend with impeccable taste in whiskey, a knack for sourcing, and an Eeyore-like persona—asks how I am. I say: I’m here. I ask how he is. He says: “I’ll be here physically, not mentally.” For all we grouse, most of us will be here for a while. In a country whose founding myth is entwined with idealism, we have our own addiction to that sort of lore. Many of us drive to a brutalist building with a broken gate to work in a profession we’re told is on life support because, despite our well-earned cynicism, we believe it matters that we try to close the gap between what it is and what it could be. The cops reporter will be here making calls about the city’s cooperation with ICE. The crime-and-court duo will dig into a cold case. This evening, my phone will buzz with a text from a source who will gleefully instruct me to watch my email next week. When we hang up, I will grab a Breathalyzer so I can report on exactly what an infamous ballpark margarita will do to my organs for a story I will write when I sober up. For now, I prowl the office in an unsuccessful search for free food. Back at my desk, I message people in a county where 119 died in last July’s floods. I check my in-box again—mostly spam—before I wave goodbye to the others and drive out past the caution-taped gate. —Sarah Smith, senior storyteller at the Houston Chronicle Overheard on Capitol Hill, seat of the government of the United States of America: “I’m here in case something crazy happens, like the raccoon penis.” Where else could this be but the Rayburn House Office Building, where health and human services secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is set to testify? He is fifteen minutes late when he saunters into the room—a brightly fluorescent-lit brown-and-blue space mostly covered in wood paneling, well-­trodden carpet, and oil paintings of past committee chairs—at 9:15 a.m., ready to talk about a gamut of quotidian topics. Meanwhile, a report from a new book on Kennedy about how he allegedly once chopped off a dead raccoon’s penis during a family road trip to “study later” has gone viral. This is the 2027 budgeting process. Kennedy is one day removed from offering a qualified embrace of the measles vaccine he had long vilified, but that isn’t stopping House Democrats from grilling him on his and Trump’s greatest hits. One of them, Representative Joe Courtney of Connecticut, invokes the legacies of Kennedy’s famous uncles to lash out at the secretary for the 1.2 million people who’ve already lost health insurance during Trump’s second term. Two others, representatives Suzanne Bonamici of Oregon and Haley Stevens of Michigan, call on Kennedy to resign “and allow someone with real qualifications to run this agency.” Kennedy never fidgets in his chair but rather scrolls through his phone and clears his throat. On the bizarre, taxpayer-funded video of his shirtless workout with Kid Rock, which featured them drinking milk in a hot tub: “So many people have come up to me who saw that video and said, ‘That inspired me to work out.’ ” On Trump demanding that Iran “open the Fuckin’ Strait, you crazy bastards, or you’ll be living in Hell”: “The president is a bargainer, and he knows how to make good deals.” On focusing more time on the presence of fluoride in water than on gun violence, the top cause of death in the United States: “You think I should be regulating gangs?” Republicans mostly lob softball questions and praise Kennedy for his inverted food pyramid and opposition to gender-affirming care for minors. But Representative Tim Walberg of Michigan, the committee chairman, asks Kennedy about critics saying he was responsible for the measles outbreak. “The accusation is not science based,” Kennedy argues. Another Republican, Representative Virginia Foxx of North Carolina, points to a recent study that found no connection between Tylenol and autism, one of Kennedy’s long-held baseless beliefs. “The study is a garbage study and should be retracted,” he replies. Then, nearly two and a half hours into a hearing that has mostly cleared out, Democratic representative Adelita Grijalva of Arizona needles Kennedy about an “affinity to perform your own medical research. “You apparently once cut the penis off a road-killed raccoon to study them later,” she says. Kennedy doesn’t fidget, and he doesn’t scoff. One hundred years from now, transcribed in the Congressional Record, this will, indeed, look crazy. But right now, it’s an utterly normal line of questioning. —Timothy Bella A very long time ago, the Lakota tribe of the Great Plains would tell a story about why it’s so damn cold in North Dakota. It goes like this: There was a giant named Wasiya that roamed the land. His clothes were made of ice, they said, and from his bonnet hung icicles. Winter came when Wasiya would blow his cold breath over the land, spreading frost and snow and wind to everything it touched. But here’s the thing about that climate: It creates some of the most productive farmland on earth. Today, roughly one fifth of America’s barley comes from North Dakota, where agriculture is a $12 billion industry. The vast majority of the work is done by family-owned businesses, like Gackle Farms in Kulm, which grows soybeans and corn and also barley, which is sold to Anheuser-Busch InBev to make Budweiser and Corona and Stella Artois and many other beers. “Winters in North Dakota are long and cold, and everything’s dead,” says Josh Gackle, fifty-one, third-generation farmer. It is 28 degrees and snowing in Kulm. And yet Josh and his brother, Jordan, forty-five, with whom he farms, can feel the onset of spring and, with it, planting season. By next week, the days will begin at 7:00 a.m. and stretch until 10:00 or 11:00 at night. The Gackles kind of obsess over the weather. “We don’t think in terms of one to two days out,” says Jordan. “We have to think in terms of five to ten days out, because if you’re not doing that, you’re not strategizing the right way to get your crop in the ground in a timely way.” This morning, like all the others before it and after, Jordan watches a thirty-minute YouTube video from a fertilizer company that breaks down the weather. It helps him understand the long-range forecast and maybe provides a little peace of mind. The weather and the integrity of the complex machines they use to do the farming—which they’re repairing today—are all anxious-making variables on the farm, but their biggest source of worry is the news. Perhaps you’ve heard it’s a tough time to be an American farmer. They rely on long-term, predictable markets for their livelihood. The president’s trade war has obliterated any degree of certainty. Plus, the price of, well, everything has gone up: fuel, seed, chemicals, land, equipment, fertilizer—especially fertilizer. The main product they use for fertilizer is urea, and guess what the ships that carry it need to pass through? Bingo: the Strait of Hormuz. When the Iranians closed it—just before planting season in the upper Midwest—fertilizer prices shot up 47 percent. You would be correct to say the president started this war at precisely the worst time for America’s farmers. “We’re in year four where net income on most farms across the country has been below the cost of production,” Josh, who also sits as a director on the board of the American Soybean Association, tells me. Jordan says the first thing he does every morning—before he looks at the weather, consults with the AI assistant Claude (financial modeling), or eats breakfast with his wife and two kids—is check the news on his phone. What are the commodity markets doing? Oil markets? He skims the headlines to see what’s happening geopolitically and then tries to determine how whatever’s going on in the Middle East or China or Washington, D.C., is going to affect decisions that need to be made on their farm. “I don’t know if it’s the earlier sunrises and the smells of spring waking me up early or it’s the stress of the war in Iran,” Jordan says, only half joking. His brother jumps in: “The markets react to a set of tweets much faster than anything we can do on the farm. And those tweets, just to be clear, come from one person.” He pauses to let the statement settle. “They’ll sink a market overnight. You wake up in the morning, you’ll wonder why soybeans are down fifty cents, and you’re like, ‘Oh, because this tweet was sent at 3:00 a.m.” For a very long time, the native people of North Dakota and, later, those who came to settle this land worried most about Wasiya’s breath. Today’s farmers have YouTube videos and also insulated Carhartt jackets and heated workshops. Life can endure on this land. It’s the country they have to worry about. And all the other things too. Now they need to stop talking to me and start driving their crop to a grain elevator before it fills up with someone else’s corn. Later, Jordan’s teenage son will stop by the Gackles’ heated shop to do a few repairs on a truck. There’s another spring rite of passage happening in Kulm: Tomorrow is prom. Jordan’s son is going to pick up his date in the truck. His dad and his uncle—two giants of North Dakota—will help him fix it up. Planting season doesn’t start until next week. —Michael Sebastian When Christina Salmon’s plane touched down at home in the Sovereign Native Village of Igiugig, trucks were waiting. Half of Igiugig, it seemed. Okay, not really. But five households and the construction company, social-services program, and student government all had pickups ready to unload supplies—no small portion of a village with maybe sixty people. Salmon was coming from Anchorage, where her daughter had to get an MRI at the Alaska Native Medical Center. She figured she might as well do some shopping while she was there. She called everyone back home to see what they might need. The damage: six Costco runs, ATV parts from Alaska Cycle Center, tools from Lowe’s and Home Depot, and seafood from New Sagaya. Friday morning she took it all to Merrill Field to be loaded onto the nine-seater plane that would take her home. All flights to Igiugig are on nine-seater planes, because that’s the legal passenger limit for a flight with one pilot. Keeps the costs down. The ticket prices can be a bear, but as groceries and sundries go, it’s not a bad deal. The first fifty pounds of luggage per person are free, and because Salmon had her daughter in tow, she had one hundred pounds to work with. She paid $470 in overages—well worth it for essentials that couldn’t be gotten at home. The flight is only about an hour and fifteen minutes, also not a bad deal. If you DoorDash pizza from Anchorage, and you time it right, it’ll reach Igiugig warm. After she touched down, the supplies were divvied among the trucks, and her own haul was home, Salmon could finally unpack. She had been away for eleven days—she met her daughter in Anchorage straight from a professional conference in Chandler, Arizona, about tribal self-governance. There, as anywhere else in the lower forty-eight, Igiugig had been a subject of fascination. “The number-one question we get is ‘You live in some small, remote village. What do you guys even do?’ ” Salmon says. “And the best way I’ve learned to describe it is: We live like every other American city, just on a very tiny, micro level.” Everything put away, she decided to stop by her youngest daughter’s school to say hi on her way into the office. —Kevin Dupzyk Under a pine tree in St. Louis, in the shadow of a brick storage shed, Bill and Chaco are halfway through another pile of golf balls, which Bill shrinks by lofting chips onto a practice green. Bill moves with the urgency of a retiree in his sixties, having worked enough Fridays to earn the peace and silence of this one, tucked in his own little corner of the public course at Forest Park. A chip lands close to his marker, a bright-red ball, and he nods to himself. The next flies wide and he winces, smiles, whispers: “Whoops.” Up the hill, a bus stops, its brakes whine, and a class unloads for a field trip to the Saint Louis Art Museum, splitting the quiet in the middle of Bill’s backswing. Over the hill, young moms flood into the Saint Louis Zoo, one of many free attractions in the city’s treasured public park, pushing their babies in overpriced strollers. It’s the start of the midday rush, when Forest Park swirls with activity, from golfing to breathing the 75 degree air. But nothing can crease Bill’s and Chaco’s rhythm, as if their morning, like every morning of Bill’s retired life, were set to the metronome of chipping balls at nothing in particular. “I don’t belong to a country club or anything like that,” he says, lining up another shot. “So to have this here, to pull up and spend zero dollars, it’s perfect. We do this a couple times a week.” “We” is Bill and Chaco. They went for a hike earlier then zipped over to Forest Park in Bill’s scratched-up CR-V, all the windows down. They do everything together. And Chaco? He’s lying in the grass, ready to move with the well-trained urgency of a border collie–Australian shepherd mix. When Bill chips the standard white balls, Chaco stays still, taking in the morning through chestnut eyes. But when Bill reaches for the lime-green ball, Chaco sits up. He’s alert and already panting. As soon as Bill connects, Chaco scrambles up the hill and fetches the ball before it stops rolling. “Good boy,” Bill says, squinting behind his dark sunglasses. “Good, good boy, Chaco.” —Jesse Dougherty My deliveries are taking longer than usual today, because there’s more people in the street—it’s day two of French Quarter Fest. I get to Fritzel’s European Jazz Pub around 10:30 a.m. I pull up in my Penske box truck out front on Bourbon Street and park. I deliver through the front door at Fritzel’s. On Fridays they start early, so there’s a jazz band playing inside already and lots of tourists and partiers who are dancing and rowdy inside. Sometimes I have to be rude to people when I’m making my way through to the bar. I say, Hey, can y’all move, to get them out of the way when I’m trying to navigate with my dolly. If they don’t move, I just start bumping into people. But what can I do, I’m working. It doesn’t matter how early in the morning it is; people are drinking already and dancing around to the band. I don’t blame them, though. My favorite part of the day is when I get to just drive around, roll down my window, and listen to the music outside. There’s bands and musicians playing on every block. I love to find the rhythm of the city in the jazz trumpets and drums that carry me through the different nooks and crannies of the Quarter. The city’s still so alive, no matter how many setbacks we’ve experienced. —TJ Sharp, delivery driver for International Wine & Spirits, as told to Sirena He Adam wakes from a dream he can’t remember in the only home he’s known since leaving his mother’s duplex five years ago. Was it the dream of baseball, a Carolina beach, or the one in which Katie, his junior high crush, is still alive? Today is Friday. A visit from high schoolers awaits, then work, followed by a baseball game with Tim, who also lives here at Champions Place, a residence for adults with physical disabilities in Roswell, Georgia, where Adam Laarhoven, age thirty-seven, faces the day. Adam has spina bifida myelomeningocele. From the waist down, he’s paralyzed. Two titanium rods in his back remind him of the surgeries as a kid that swallowed whole summers. What would you do? A kid in summer? You too would watch the Braves and idolize Chipper Jones and wear his number 10 in wheelchair handball for the YMCA Mustangs and scowl like Chipper, throw and score like Chipper, season after season, and when the Braves caravan swings by the YMCA, it feels fated when out steps Chipper. Don’t meet your heroes? Hardly. Challenge them to air hockey. A 2–2 tie. And when Chipper Jones smiles and extends his hand, you shake hands and call it good. Today, Adam reaches for his favorite Braves hat—a kelly-green Saint Patrick’s Day limited edition. “For good luck,” he says and smiles. The high schoolers bring trivia, subs from Jersey Mike’s, and homemade chocolate-chip cookies. Adam knows the students by name; he asks about their prom plans and playlists and shares his dance stories. For three years they have been visiting Champions Place and weeks ago volunteered at a wheelchair-sports scrimmage. The kids pushed residents and other wheelchair athletes from Metro Atlanta. Today they present personalized superlative awards to each resident. Cheers ring out as Alyson is named the Sage, Tim the All-Rounder, and Luke the Human Emoji. Adam’s superlative? Captain America. The room erupts as he steers to the front. Over the past two years, Adam has had surgeries for pressure sores and kidney stones, bone and urinary-tract infections. The original Captain America suffered from a litany of ailments, but a serum transformed him into a supersoldier. The kids tell Adam his superpower is connecting and inspiring. Adam is aware; he’s been told this before. He’s also aware of our tendency to sanctify the disabled with ready-made halos. “But I want to inspire,” he says and adjusts the bill of his cap. “I’d be like this no matter what.” Like what? “A people person.” When the Atlanta Braves moved to the strip malls of Smyrna, they made parking hell. So Adam and Tim stick to the local high school hardball circuit for their ball game. Tim knows the inning, the outs, the count. Adam? He’s making his rounds—catching up with friends, waving to familiar faces, and meeting folks in the concession’s long line. Night falls as the minivans round the corner back to Champions Place. Adam tells Tim goodnight and heads to his room. The certificate from the high schoolers sits next to his heavy row of Mustang medals, his granddad’s dog tags from Vietnam, and yellow rosary beads. Adam hangs up his hat across from a framed picture he keeps of him and Katie, back when they were teens; she died from spina bifida complications as an adult. There is no serum for spina bifida. No serum exists for the requisite courage and grace to be a people person. No serum is currently available to recover lost dreams. But here’s one. There is a land where all people are endowed with the human right to health care and a home. A land of plenty where everyone pitches in to build places like this across a country that we may one day call America. —Jeremy Collins It’s a beautiful spring day in Philadelphia at a spot on Market Street called Franklin Court, a historic area where Ben Franklin’s house was. There is a mulberry tree in the garden that became the place where he met with family and people like Thomas Jefferson and other dignitaries. When I’m under that tree, I am Benjamin Franklin. I’ve been an actor for fifty years. When I moved here twenty-six years ago, I saw historic actors and wanted to be one. In 2002, when I no longer had to go to a day job, I got involved with Historic Philadelphia Inc., a nonprofit that brings history to life through storytelling. I was told, “Hey, you should be Benjamin Franklin.” Today, I’m doing a little rehearsal. It’s not scripted. It’s all very first-person. I’m all about engaging with visitors just as Dr. Franklin would have done. I wear clothing that’s authentic for the time. Cotton, linen, wool. I had a bad experience years ago with a dry cleaner that shrank my whole outfit; there’s only one that I will use, which also does things for the Phillies and Eagles. I used to wear a wig, but I don’t anymore. It’s a lot easier with my own hair, and I don’t have little children saying, “That looks like a wig!” Kids will ask, “What are the names of your brothers and sisters?” I would say, well, my parents had seventeen children. I was the fifteenth. I don’t even remember some of them. There was an older brother, my father’s namesake, Josiah, who went off to sea before I was born and never came back. The first thing I ever wanted to do growing up was to be a sailor. And my parents said, “We’re not losing another son to the sea.” If I wanted to get really gory, I could talk about another brother who died before I was born. He was nineteen months old and drowned in the suds from my father’s soap business. I try not to tell the children that one. Only the really curious adults. It’s a little early yet, but people are starting to come. I had a wonderful conversation with a couple from St. Louis who are retired schoolteachers, fans of history. A couple of tour groups came through as well. Although I don’t have an exclusive franchise to be Ben Franklin, I think I’ve been well received. People seem to still hire me. —Bill Robling, Ben Franklin Interpreter, as told to Chris Hatler Most people don’t know where Baxter Springs, Kansas, is, let alone think of it as a “border town.” But there it sits, right on the border of Kansas and Oklahoma, a stone’s throw from Missouri. Oklahoma was not a state until 1907. Prior to that it was Oklahoma Territory, and prior to that, it went by “Indian Territory.” Border towns, or former border towns, often cropped up as close as possible to Indian (re)settlements, ideal trade locations. Route 66 runs right through this history and main-street Baxter as it runs from Kansas into Oklahoma. After the federal government crushed Indigenous subsistence economies, federal dollars flowed into those locations along with unscrupulous settlers who were given trade monopolies over tribes. Remnants of this history are subtly visible if you look for clues left along the route. However, if you look only for the narratives of yesteryear, you will have hamstrung your trip. Not long after we crossed the border into Oklahoma, we pulled into the Quapaw Farmers Market and were greeted by Quapaw Tribal member Brittany Dixon. We pulled bison flank steaks from the freezer as Brittany chatted us up. The bison on the counter waiting to be rung up came from the Quapaw Nation’s herd and was processed at the Quapaw Nation’s slaughterhouse. When we told her we were doing a little bit for Esquire, she suddenly became bashful. As I held up my camera, another Brittany came out of nowhere to show off her relative’s earrings. The Brittanys ran my card, laughed at us, and generously sent us on our way with some freezer packs. If you have never experienced the sweet music of two Native women cackling and laughing, you ain’t lived, and you ain’t crossed enough borders. —Ryan RedCorn (Osage/𐓷𐓘𐓻𐓘𐓻𐓟) Read Esquire’s coverage of the 100th anniversary of Route 66 here. Mr. Beef opens at 10:00, but it doesn’t really matter when you go. There will be a line. Today fifteen young men show up, maybe a little after 11:00, with fifteen suitcases. Bachelor party. They’d come straight from the plane. The devotion has something to do with the food, the Italian beef drowning in jus—usually ordered “wet” so the bun is too—and topped with giardiniera, the kind of heavy lunch that sustains the kind of people who gave Chicago its Big Shoulders, the kind of people who built the Midwest. But it’s also to do with The Bear, which is set in a fictionalized version of Mr. Beef. As the lunch hour proceeds, maybe 75 percent of the people in the prodigious line are fans of Carmy, Fak, the whole Berzatto crew. But the balance may shift later in the year. The heavily tatted man manning the register, loud and very Chicago—yes, this would be Cousin—says you should see the place in summer. “We are nonstop for the entire day,” he says. “You’re here on a slow day.” —Lyndon French The ferry to Ellis Island was a symphony: Spanish, French, English in every accent, languages I couldn’t place. The forty-five-minute ride felt long, but it couldn’t compare with the journeys people once made to get here. My great-grandparents came from Eastern Europe. This ferry was nothing. I had come, as so many do, hoping to see my family’s records—the real notes immigration officers took upon greeting them: age, national origin, destination, languages spoken, where they would stay. I’d found them online, but I wanted to see them in the flesh, in the place they were written. When I got there, I learned the Records Discovery Center was closed for construction, along with much of the museum. A small wrench in the plans. Still, I stayed. The Great Hall, where hundreds of immigrants once crowded together under a magnificent arched, tiled ceiling, was draped in brown tarps. I passed an Orthodox Jewish family speaking Hebrew and wondered whether their family had come from the shtetls of Eastern Europe, like mine. I watched a short film about the immigration process. “Going to America then was like going to the moon now,” it said. My great-grandmother came at twenty-one, by herself. My great-grandfather was on the island for a long time, quarantined with an eye infection. He was thirteen. I hadn’t seen their records, but I realized that wasn’t exactly what I’d come for after all. The ferry back to Manhattan again echoed with languages that filled the Great Hall a hundred years ago. For a long time, that hall was the way into this country. Now it’s a museum. —Bellamy Richardson It’s an eerily quiet afternoon at the Somerset Diner, which sits in the town of the same name at the corner of John F. Kennedy Boulevard and Easton Ave. It’s one of fifteen twenty-four-hour diners operating in New Jersey. Is it not a quintessential part of the American dream for a couple of guys to get a meal at any hour they please? There used to be many more of these institutions that characterized my great state, but since Covid-19, they’ve inched toward extinction. I guess the world couldn’t handle such a grand idea. I can’t actually remember the last time I had lunch with my best friend. Just lunch, just the two of us. It might have been 2013. We would have been seniors at Rutgers, meeting between classes. In the years since graduation, Andrew—which isn’t his real name—pinballed across America in pursuit of his Ph.D. in marine biology. He spent the bulk of his twenties in Louisiana before waiting out the pandemic in California. I, on the other hand, planted roots on the east coast, working in an industry that treats the New York metro area as the center of the world. Three years ago, Andrew got married, got his doctorate, and came home to Jersey. It was mainly for personal reasons—to be closer with family and because the pizza and bagels are second to none. He stayed for professional reasons. He works for a research lab at our alma mater. While we’ve had dinners and boozy game nights since his return, we haven’t grabbed lunch. Over a mountain of beef-chili nachos, pork-roll cheeseburgers, and bottomless coffee, we catch up. The families are good, everyone’s healthy. He tells me what his dog chewed through last week. That government funding is drying up for academic research. I tell him journalists are losing ground to influencers. That my niece is in kindergarten. He updates me on his wife. I tell him about my latest Bumble disaster. We bemoan five-dollar gas and trade progress on our Magic: The Gathering decks. It’s the best afternoon I’ve had in months. Then Andrew tells me he’s been bricking his phone. What? “It’s a little NFC reader that is magnetically attached. You open the app and you brick your phone,” he explains. Apparently it’s a thing for a lot of other people. “Like, I don’t want to look at Instagram. So you brick it, and there is, honest to God, nothing you can do.” Andrew can access the phone for emergency situations but only five times. Ever. It has been two weeks of this, and he says it’s changed his life. “Being present is the key,” he says. What has he received in return? Time. Sanity. Focus. “It’s given me my autonomy back. I’ve been walking more than ever. I go outside.” I probe him further on his self-imposed blackout. As a journalist who must always know what’s happening all the time, I’m curious and frankly envious of the mental and emotional space he’s afforded himself. He’s honest with me. It was his own desperate need to sever himself from algorithms that made him stare into the regressive political and cultural abyss. Andrew and I both are second-generation immigrants. He laments that whatever prosperity drew both our parents to America in the first place has all but vanished. He mentions a mutual friend, another research scientist, whose work was not receiving funding here. She met a guy in Sweden, where her work receives better financial support, and is openly considering a move abroad. “It feels like we went from a land of opportunity to opportunities missed,” Andrew says. We are the lucky ones. We’re holding down careers we wanted for ourselves, leading lives we set out to have. Mostly. He suffered a deep bout of depression in the months between earning his Ph.D. and landing his current job. I am, regrettably, swiping on the apps, living in a one-bedroom I can just barely afford. These are our dreams, but they’re not, and never will be, perfect. Sometimes they’re everything but. That’s why it’s crucial to have a friend you can count on. Even if it’s just for lunch. —Eric Francisco On this very day, Allegheny General Hospital straddles the past and the future, the old and the new. It was built nearly a century ago on top of a hill overlooking downtown Pittsburgh. These walls saw smoke from the steel mills rise and disappear, framing out a country, giving way to Roberto Clemente, Andy Warhol, Rachel Carson, the Steel Curtain, Mister Rogers’ Neighborhood. Now The Pitt makes scalding, primordial drama of this newest chapter in the life of this American city—medicine, public health, hospitals, emergency care. A promise. The monster-hit show is set right here. Its leading figure, Noah Wyle’s Dr. Robby, considered the end of his life from the edge of its rooftop. He travels as much as this work allows—with his wife and, increasingly, his two kids—and he’s jet-lagged from his flight back from Hong Kong. They know about The Pitt there. He showed off his picture with Noah Wyle. Dr. Rau isn’t practicing today, but he stops by the emergency department for a look. The space is undergoing a $43 million renovation, and it’s halfway finished. He walks past tape, plywood, warning signs, and right into a huddle of charge nurses. All hell broke loose when you were gone, someone jokes. A man in black scrubs welcomes him back. You’re never truly free of this place. Dr. Rau was at a birthday party when the hospital called him and said someone opened fire at a Trump rally in nearby Butler County and victims were coming their way. The doctor knows this place will outlive him. This system is fucked, and you may be fucked too, but if you enter that door, he will take care of you. Enter the door of any emergency department in this country, you will receive care. He remembers the name of every child he’s pronounced dead. Those younger than eighteen. Especially under five. What he suspects will live with him, long after he leaves, is the sound of a mother screaming for her baby. The beginning of Covid, when patients walked in the door—they were blue, hypoxic, the oxygen draining from every cell of their body. A day when an elderly patient fell, hit their head, and died from the bleeding. In the neighboring room, a woman giving birth. Same time. Dr. Rau looks at the ground, at the point where two different floorings converge, smack in the middle of the renovation. A shady two-mile stretch of the lower river, back away from the highway, through tall firs and alders. Our friend Mike who lives upriver says they just stocked this section with a ton of fish. Mike has this beautiful old aluminum drift boat built in the seventies by Bruce Koffler, a legendary boatbuilder out by the airport in Eugene. It’s the early modern version of the McKenzie River drift boat invented right here in the 1920s and ’30s. An agile, steeply rockered design with narrow ends, usually fourteen to sixteen feet long with eight- or nine-foot oars. It navigates heavy rapids with grace but has enough glide to hold you still within the flow so you can cast your tiny fly out and land it gently on the water. “You wanna row?” Mike says, without waiting for an answer. “I’m happy when other people row, so I can fish.” I pull the anchor rope, grab the oars, and ferry out into the current. Mike gets two bites right away and lands one. A tiny rainbow. Mike had a wooden boat before the fire in 2020, which also took his house and about four hundred other homes in the McKenzie Valley. A tree hit a power pole upriver about twenty-five miles from here. It burned more than 170,000 acres, one of the largest wildfires in Oregon history. The winds that day blew the fire down the canyon so fast that Mike and his wife, Jean, had to evacuate with little notice, escaping with little more than two of Mike’s guitars and a six-pack of Corona. They lost everything. The river upstream still runs clear and beautiful, but it’s no longer as shady as it is down here. The fire-scarred trees that line that stretch still fall sometimes, but most at this point are going to make it. Homes are now rebuilt—though not for the poorer residents and the many underinsured who’ve been forced to move on. Most things aren’t as good as they were, but some things are better. The burned-over hazelnut farms that line the river here are replanted. And the price of nuts is up. Mike got the son of the contractor who built his old house to build him a new one. And now he has this new old boat, one of the first ones Bruce Koffler ever built. He picked it up for thirteen hundred bucks with the trailer. What a deal. Now Mike’s on the oars so I can fish. I brace myself in the front of the boat and work my line into loops overhead, shooting it out toward likely-looking holes as we drift past, but nothing’s working. A March Brown, a golden stonefly nymph, a heavy conehead woolly bugger. I keep looking deeper. We shouldn’t have left that first spot, we keep saying. The water is a little too high and pushy today. The trout are all slammed down on the bottom, hiding out behind the rocks. I grab my spinning rod and toss a purple rooster tail into a deep, swirling eddy line behind a gravel bar. “This looks like a good spot to me.” Mike says. The rod bends. It does to me too. —Tom Colligan Tyshawn Lamar Jones Jr. got his love of horses from his pop-pop. Jones grew up mostly in the suburbs, not a prime location for horses, but one day Pop-pop, Johnny Avon Middleton, introduced him to one. He was smitten with the animal, smitten with the whole thing: grooming the horse, calming it, “breathing the air in and just seeing such a beautiful creature.” He’s been working in barns for a few years now, and this year he started at Thunder Ridge Farms, where he also lives. At Thunder Ridge they board horses and teach riding classes to people of all ages and skill levels. Jones’s job is to tend the horses. This morning he fed them, now he’s riding them to burn off energy before they go back in the barn, and this evening he’ll feed them again. “Knowing you can make a twelve-hundred-pound animal do what you ask,” he says, having stood calmly atop one, “the connection, the bond, the trust you gain with them—it’s something you just can’t explain.” All you can do—and this is something he does, if someone asks, even though he doesn’t formally teach classes—is what his pop-pop did for him: put a person on a horse, teach them balance, how to hold the reins, how to make sure their heel is down. “A horse can brighten a day in ways you can’t imagine,” Jones says. —Evgeny Popov Over half of A-dorm’s ninety-six men take the opioid substitute Suboxone as part of ADOC’s drug-treatment program. The overseers here spend a tremendous amount of time and resources trying to convince, mold, and shape all of us into believing that we are the same. I wear the same clothes, eat the same food, and live in the same dorm as my peers, but inwardly I know I am different. Being the same represents a form of civil death to me. Hopefully not to Hollywood. —Anthony Breznican There isn’t a lot of evidence of the distant past left in San Francisco, which has famously been wracked by earthquakes and, more importantly, did something quite strange in 1900—outsource eternity. The Board of Supervisors forbade burials in the geographically constrained City by the Bay and eventually exhumed some 125,000 corpses to make space. They were reburied in nearby Colma, a vast necropolis to the south. You can still buy T-shirts there reading, “It’s great to be alive in Colma.” But Mission Dolores still has its cemetery, and on this afternoon, mission curator Andrew Galvan is leading a tour. In tow are three dozen docents from missions up and down California, visiting for the 43rd Annual California Missions and Presidios Conference. Mission Dolores: a Catholic church of Spanish-colonial provenance that celebrated its first mass five days before the Declaration of Independence was signed. For Californians of a certain age, creating sugar-cube dioramas of missions was a de rigueur elementary school project. “We try to discourage that today,” says Galvan, the descendant of Ohlone Indians who were rounded up and taken here against their will. “We don’t have students make dioramas of concentration camps when they’re studying World War II or plantations when they’re discussing slavery. We’re really trying to dispel the romantic story of California’s missions and tell the true story.” The curator’s great-great-great-great-grandparents, Faustino and Obulinda, lie below, along with almost fourteen thousand others. The cemetery used to be significantly larger than its current small fraction of a city block: Mission Dolores, like San Francisco, repurposed its burial grounds. “If you parked in our schoolyard,” Galvan says with a nod toward distant ballplayers shooting hoops, “you probably parked on top of somebody.” Everybody gasps. And then they laugh. It’s a bit surprising in San Francisco, where future generations can get just as little deference as those past. A block away at Dolores Park, like the mission named for a buried creek, a quick count of the north section reveals twenty-one dogs and just one child. The city has long had the lowest percentage of children in not just the United States but all of North America. In the park a man attempts a handstand on a yoga mat while four young men in tank tops play spikeball; Johnny Griffin’s 1956 rendition of “These Foolish Things” wafts out of their Bluetooth speaker. Dolores Park, too, was once a cemetery. But on this Friday, the past, like the future, is out of sight and out of mind. It is a serene spring evening, and all are enjoying the glorious present. —Joe Eskenazi By April most of the cruise ships have left San Juan Harbor for the Mediterranean, even though the weather here remains pleasant. So on a day like this, after coffee shops close and the remaining tourists embark, the streets of El Viejo San Juan belong once again to the locals—locals like Angie and Alfonso, a couple in their late sixties—and to the memories that linger here. Today it is not too hot, not too windy. Angie’s hat did not fly away. This often happens with the winds around Castillo San Felipe del Morro, where Angie and Alfonso started their afternoon walk, a walk they take often. The old city—the oldest under U.S. jurisdiction, as Alfonso clarifies—is clean and well maintained, they note. Angie is notoriously the first to comment about trash on sidewalks or when places look in decay. She’s political in that way and would quickly post about it on Facebook, whereas Alfonso tends to have a nostalgic allegiance. But both would agree that the narrow streets and architecture are a “treasure gifted from the past.” It sounds more poetic in Spanish. They pass by La Iglesía San José and sit in the plaza that overlooks it. Alfonso, a history buff who excels at occasionally useless trivia, recounts how the remains of Juan Ponce de León, Puerto Rico’s first governor, who later named Florida, were there before being moved to La Catedral down the street. In churches Angie simply finds solemnity. This one is the second oldest in the Americas. They got married here in 1982; they will celebrate their forty-fourth anniversary this June. These streets, or adoquines, far predate their marriage and their lives. Even in colonial times they were known for their festivals and for witnessing miracles. Angie’s grandmother, a San Juanera, was born here and loved sharing ghost stories. Angie’s favorite concerned a jockey somehow surviving his horse’s fall off a ledge after a race. It took place in La Calle del Cristo, which connects the church where she got married and La Catedral, where Juan Ponce de León is now buried and where Alfonso was baptized in 1956. He recalls waiting on its steps to take his son home from SanSe, the annual street festival that serves as a rite of passage for most Puerto Rican teenagers—aka their first drink of many. Alfonso chuckles, remembering how he escaped there when he was younger, hinting at his own debauchery. Rounding the corner, they turn onto Calle Fortaleza, home to the oldest executive mansion in continuous use in the U.S., near where Alfonso lived as a newborn—and where Angie feels connected to ancestors and a family no longer there. She points out an apartment where a cannonball hit her maternal great-grandparents’ balcony during the Spanish–American War in 1898. Thankfully there were no casualties, but it’s fair to say that the balcony no longer exists. Next they walk by Angie’s father’s father’s old storefront. He imported European furniture, hardware supplies that were hard to find at the time, and even German toys. Good toys, she says. Alfonso eagerly steers them down La Calle Tetuán, where his father owned a business when Old San Juan was the commercial capital of the island. They can’t help themselves, sharing fond memories across generations of places and tales of loved ones no longer here. And what of the future? Now they loudly debate, rather excitedly and too eagerly, about what time they’ll pick me up from the SJU airport when I return for the holidays, when Angie will decorate the house and wait to see what I notice, while Alfonso heads straight to the kitchen to prepare me an egg breakfast—a Spanish tortilla or rustic eggs in a cazuela. The son at SanSe, I should say, was me. Angie and Alfonso are my parents, and I am a loved one who is no longer here. I ask my parents to share these stories with me as it’s been sixteen years since I moved to the continental U.S. and left my precious island. The longer I’ve been away, the more I long for a spoken history unknown to any textbook. It can be lonely even in New York City, just a three-and-a-half-hour flight away. Many Americans question Puerto Ricans’ worth—even our Super Bowl halftime performance. I question the history classes that failed to teach them. Many insist on botching our language and misinterpreting our culture. My identity gets questioned; my passport too. But hearing my parents recount our rich history, one older than the U.S., a history that dates further back than 1776 but takes place in America, I am reminded that we are American, but maybe we’ll say it in Spanish: americano. —Alfonso Fernández Navas Everything broke for the better by evening. The rain had blasted through Harvey’s oyster farm in late afternoon, in drenching sheets and gusts that whipped against the dock and shoreline. It stung my face before ending as abruptly as it began. Bluff Hill Cove, one of the jewels of Point Judith Pond in Narragansett, Rhode Island, turned calm. I walked to the end of the dock, pulled a mossy line, and lifted a floating crate from the flowing tide. Oysters my son Mick had raised rattled inside. Winter had been brutally cold, so frigid that the pond’s saltwater surface froze solid for weeks. The oysters and clams beneath that snow-covered lid were dormant. But with the slowly warming temperatures of April, shellfish were waking up. I picked an oyster from Mick’s stash. The leading edge of its two shells, away from the hinge, showed a faint white crust resembling a fingernail – the telltale sign of fresh growth. A flash of satisfaction moved me. At last the natural new year was upon us. It was audible too, in the songs of a yellow-rumped warbler, a red-winged blackbird, an American robin, all migratory birds freshly returned, flitting through the sumac along the shore. Oyster aquaculture and wild-clam harvesting are demanding. But with their natural rhythms, their body-straining labors, and their succulent results, they serve as counterpoints and complements to the intensities that play out onshore. Here you are on nature’s calendar and clock, governed by weather, season, and tides, which together are a blend of the predictable and the seemingly random. We entered the game as a family when the kids were smaller, a way to access healthy local food and the work of the water together. Now it’s a business, helping Mick pay his medical school bills while employing other watermen and providing fresh raw oysters to markets that a generation of farmers before us—Harvey and Tom—opened with vision and hard work. Now there is work for our family: a used skiff motor to swap out for a less used replacement, oyster cages to haul, crop to inspect, new seed to put into the water for the seven months of growth before cold slows it all down again. Mick will lead. I will follow. Other sons will join. My phone vibrates in my pocket. Friday evening. I flinch a little. It could be any number of day-job bosses asking boss things. I took to the water in part to slip away from people, politics, priorities, and pain I do not pretend to understand. Here on Bluff Hill Cove Oyster Farm, or just up the channel at the North Star lease, life is better. Damn that phone. I retrieve it from my pocket to check. It’s the best boss of all: my father, in his eighty-eighth year. “Are the oysters growing again now?” he asks by text. I smile. We’re in rhythm, and maybe, just maybe, like the weather at this moment, everything will be okay. —Chris Chivers We got a call as soon as the evening shift started. There was a preemie baby at a hospital who needed to be transferred to the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit at the University of Michigan. The flight is quick, only about fifty minutes long, but it required a ton of preparation. A NICU call is different from what we do normally. A premature baby is so small they can’t regulate their body temperature, so we have to bring an Isolette, which is a device that helps monitor and regulate the baby’s temperature. We also have to load up special equipment like airway tools small enough to fit them, as well as medications. Everything goes onto the helicopter. It was a pretty packed crew: our pilot, me, another flight nurse, plus a NICU nurse, a NICU nurse practitioner, and a NICU respiratory therapist. Most of us had never met each other before. When we landed, the helipad wasn’t right next to the hospital, so we had to take an ambulance to pick up the baby. That meant transferring the Isolette to the ambulance. It’s heavy—it took six of us to move it. I mixed IV drips to connect to the machine, which would keep the baby alive during the flight. This kid was only around five or six pounds. It was tiny. When we got back to the helicopter, the security guy had to help us lift the Isolette back in. I began checking the inverter switch on the helicopter. We’re pessimists. It’s really important that power is running to fuel the Isolette and all the medical equipment. After takeoff, I started running through worst-case scenarios in my head, but the infant’s heart rate, temperature, and blood pressure were maintaining stability. When we were about twenty minutes out, I called in a report to our hospital and gave the dispatcher the infant’s main diagnosis and informed them of the IV drips and medications the infant was on so they could prepare for our arrival. We touched down just about midnight, and then we did the whole thing all over again to get the baby inside. The baby made it just fine. Usually by the time I show up, the family has exhausted all their other efforts. When they see me, they get fresh hope. The looks on the faces of the patient’s family are always unforgettable. —Katelyn Dykas, survival flight nurse with Michigan Medicine, as told to Sirena He It’s another night in a spring that’s coming to Boston piecemeal, one day at a time, only during the daylight hours.
