Pierce In my memory, our table was inside which it couldn’t have been. There are no dining tables inside only outside in the garden, hung with silk Fortuny lamps and insulated from the improbably close Sunset Strip by a dense cage of ivy. It’s French Gothic except you are drinking a margarita. To an Angeleno, the exoticism of a French combination bistro-­palace is consonant with the exoticism of a margarita. When my friend and her boyfriend eventually propose having a threesome with me, I am already on the brink of nausea from afternoon tamales, Mexican Japanese fusion tuna tartare, and margaritas. I lunge toward the bathroom nearly ruffling the velvet cape of Anya, the notorious doorkeeper of the hotel, as she applies her famous red lipstick. Sometimes you are the girl taking pictures in the mirror while another is throwing up, sometimes it’s the other way around. Fifteen minutes later I tell the couple I can’t stay. I do not go home but upstairs where I order a bottle of Champagne and two glasses which I take to the pool. No one stops me walking barefoot with an open bottle through the halls, or wearing only my cotton underwear and no bra in the water. In fact there is no one around at all. Lyric was the tallest of our friends and it would fall to her to stand on the bamboo chair at the entrance to the pool and reach over for the handle when we were teenagers. This was back when you could still get away with this. When there was parking on Sunset and my brother’s tae kwon do class shared a lot with the Googie bank across the street that has since been demolished. No one ever stopped us or questioned that we should be there, jettisoning the most stylish black outfits we could come up with across the damp bricks. If we wanted to appear as believable hotel guests we could have worn sweatpants and swimsuits, but this never occurred to our teenage minds. At sixteen I was often wearing a men’s black silk kimono with my mom’s borrowed couture slacks from the nineties. Lyric had her mom’s sparkly beaded Fendi purse. We would get bottles of St-Germain and sparkling water from Bogie’s Liquors in Hancock Park, where we went to school; I photoshopped a scan of my passport to say 1986 because I was the best at drawing. We made French 75’s with elderflower instead of gin because gin was gross. Looking back at the final ten years of Lyric’s life—from those nights skinny-dipping in the pool, to when I am vomiting from a broken heart and the night is still quiet and vacant, though they have removed the glass balls from the potted plants surrounding the pool, and replaced the dingy navy-and-white-striped lounge chairs with twice as many pristine pastel ones—we felt so old. Older than our parents could have possibly imagined we’d be by sixteen, having not anticipated the Internet. We knew everything. Our history, like California’s, like the hotel’s, is one after ourselves. The simulation becomes real with enough repetition, and now we really are thirty-year-old women, the tallest of us is dead, and this fantastical place to which our parents came to make movies with only their own made-up future before them has actually become our home, a place to which we return with nostalgia for a past both real and imagined. Tonight I’m sitting in the lobby, building paper airplanes with the owner’s son. Violet, Nathalie, and I are going to an astrology-themed birthday party later dressed as Virgo, Taurus, and Scorpio. “This a stealth bomber, and this one is for stunts.” We are throwing the paper airplanes toward the guy who is buying up empty lots to flip in the Palisades burn zone. It’s April now, everyone says there will be no more rain. —Mercedes Kilmer I’m a touch lightheaded but not enough to dull the electric guitar from the live music or the gnats buzzing in my ear. Very sophisticated. I’ll be going to law school at William and Mary in the fall, so I suppose it’s time to start raising the bar. The Lambda hands me fine china—a green Solo cup—and I take a sip. The wine is dry and not very good. The bass from outside thrums, causing my lightheadedness to build into a migraine. I curse myself for getting older. Conveniently, Simer laments about having to use the bathroom. Because one person has to pee, suddenly everyone does. I begin down the hall, pushing open random doors. Eventually one gives way to a bathroom. Over one of the urinals is a poster of a blond woman taking off her glasses and looking down at, well, you know. I snap a photo to laugh at later. The girls and I each take turns in the stall as my head pulses. Hannah watches me wince each time the door slams shut and suggests we go somewhere quiet. We’ve been best friends long enough for her to know I get these chronically. We find a set of rocking chairs outside on the lawn and sit. We people-watch in silence until we finish what’s inside our fine china. These weeks will be filled with many lasts. The last strawberry-acai refresher from the Tate Student Center Starbucks, the last time braving the million-dollar staircase on North Campus, my last sorority date night, last bar crawl, and, today, my last fraternity darty. I’m on the precipice of a new chapter. I don’t know what it has in store for me. I’ve been through a pandemic, and AI is coming. What I do know is I’ll never be this young again, surrounded by all the people I love at once. I glance down at my phone to check my boyfriend’s location. I’d texted him from the bathroom that I was ready to be picked up; he’s now two minutes away. “Do any of y’all need a ride downtown or back to the apartment?” I ask. Hannah turns to me, her smile flickering briefly. “I think we’re going to stay a bit longer.” —Gabrielle Gruszynski, UGA ’26 Professional Bull Riders, based in Fort Worth, Texas, has brought the classic cowboy sport to the world. Events are viewed in more than seventy countries. Keyshawn Whitehorse, a proud rider from the Navajo Nation in southeast Utah and the 2018 PBR Rookie of the Year, told Esquire’s Josh Rosenberg about taking on bull Kinky Brahmer at a PBR event in Billings. I take a good look at the animal. Really stare it in the eye. There’s a lot you can glean before a ride. My bull tonight is big for his age. It takes a little while for a young bull to get seasoned. So I can already tell that Brahmer is dangerous. If I have my foot in the wrong spot, he’ll crush it before the gates even open. The music cuts through my focus. “If you want a cowboy on a white horse ... hold on tight, girl.” My walk-on song: “White Horse” by Chris Stapleton. I didn’t choose it—the tour did, probably because of my name—but I guess you could say it’s been good to me. I’ve qualified for the World Finals eight times. But tonight, Brahmer won’t make reaching a ninth easy. I’m already hot when I enter the chute. Most people probably wouldn’t expect that from just an eight-second bull ride. But when the adrenaline kicks in, you build up a good bit of sweat. I say my prayers, hop on, the gate opens, and he kicks immediately. You’re just reacting. A wrong move made with intention will put you in a better place than a right move made in hesitation. I dig my knees into his side to check him, but he takes a bigger leap than I expect. I’m rocked to the back. And once you’re rocked to the back, you’re in the one area you want to stay away from. They call it the bull’s territory. There’s nothing in the world like it. Some bulls are just rock-solid. All muscle. When you’re a split second late you get bucked off—hard. I’m thrown after five seconds, and he leaves me behind. Tonight, the bull wins. I’m chasing a rematch now. When you respect the animal and conquer the fear, a ride makes you feel on top of the world. Cassy, a sixty-one-year-old platinum-blond dancer in black garters, wraps matches around her nipples as Taylor Swift’s “Style” plays. Within seconds, her areolas are illuminated by flames. She sways her hips on the stage behind the oval bar. “Fire tits!” shouts a dude young enough to be her grandson. A middle-aged dentist from out of town watches the flames peter out. She’s nursing a canned cocktail, smiling so wide her eyes crinkle beneath her black bob. Cassy gyrates to her side of the bar. The stripper places a mini basketball hoop in front of her crotch. The dentist balls up a dollar bill. Swish! “She looks like my mom!” the dentist shouts, then throws back her head, laughing. Her sour mood has evaporated. So has the memory of the heated argument with her husband that occurred just before. She doesn’t know where he is and doesn’t care. After one more dance, Cassy gathers the singles scattered across the stage, tosses them into a bucket, and lowers herself to the floor. Lexi, heavily pregnant, follows Cassy. Lizzo’s “Truth Hurts” pumps through the speakers as she emerges in a scarlet fishnet bra-and-pantie set that matches her curly red hair. Her Lucite heels are much shorter than other dancers’ stilettos—but that’s her only concession to pregnancy. She lowers her bra, grabs her left boob, and shimmies with the grace of a woman without a fetus inside of her. A married couple eye Lexi from their barstools. They’re celebrating their anniversary. “We’re white-trash people,” says the mid-thirties husband in hipster glasses. “We thought, Let’s have a beer and look at boobs.” His wife, in her early twenties with blond-streaked hair, feels like she can be herself here. “I don’t have to flash a bunch of money and look a certain way,” she says. Her mom was a stripper, her dad a strip-club bouncer. He sat at these same stools and watched some of the same strippers, like sixty-­nine-year-old Blondie, who follows Lexi. “My dad told me stories about her crushing beer cans with her tits,” she says. —Hallie Lieberman Leaving for the casino by day is what I call “going to church.” It’s my personal none-of-your-business assertion of an important ritual journey: a cold bottle of water, a drive through the urban wreckage at the fringe of a city, over hill and dale, betwixt river and mountain, to the business of a casino. It’s a spare pilgrimage built for a Friday afternoon, when the casino plays the part of a cathedral beckoning from a distance. Churches have only a couple of appointed entrances, and casinos too have just three main doorways max. Inside? No real windows in either, at least none you can see through clearly. Churches filter and recolor the intrusion of the outside world through occluded glass so it falls on the pews like dewy light from above. Casinos block the outside world with a wall of sound and scorching colors cast from row upon row of slot machines, built with sixty-five-inch HD televisions tilted on their ends. I like to tell myself both places are like that by design, to help with focus and self-reflection. So, yeah, on this day, like a good American, I go to church. And now I’m riding midnight again, after the usual exercises—hours in the poker room, one not-so-lucky half hour at the roulette wheel, another long stab at a slot machine, where I hit a single jackpot that righted all the wrongs that came to me as the night fell. All of it has left me, twenty minutes from midnight, more or less even for the day. When I sit down at another fifty-dollar blackjack table, I can finally see the energy of the place from my seat. The joint skews young and thumping, mad-jammed with the aforementioned light and sound, overpopulated by payday pilgrims of the dice, with throngs of date-night couples decked in their Friday best, cleavage and biceps at the ready, and roving herds of bachelor and bachelorette parties looking to stampede the night in search of its limits. There are five people sitting at the felt curl of the table when I finally take the last seat on the left, while the dealer shuffles. “How’s this been going?” I quietly ask the beefy dude to my right, a thirty-ish guy wearing an anachronistic black blazer over a bejeweled wifebeater with the exclamation “Caliente” pointed in sequins. He doesn’t answer, and the five measly chips stacked on the felt at his gut line pretty much tell the story. A bad run. He’s caught in a head shake/death stare at the apparition of luck in the space in front of him. In fact, I can see then that everyone at the table looks a bit shaggy and unnerved by their last round of cards. Not one word from any of them. There it was. That was the sign. I see that now. End of day, when midnight falls. I should have stood right up and vamoosed. Should have been happy to be even, rather than flummoxed by the fact and willing to take more. But I was night-blind at that point, locked in on the sense that I might be there to reset the luck—to bring the luck, even. You know the story from here. Like a robot of faith I drop two chips onto the table seven times, I double up twice, and the house hits blackjack twice in a row. I then double my original bet, we push on twenty twice, before I bust on thirteen. Eight straight hands. Just like that, I have tilted and tithed nine hundred bucks to the Indian nation that runs the joint. When I finally unlock my own death stare at luck from the space before me, I look around the table and register. Beefy Caliente is long gone, the others too. I stand up to go as the table repopulates with young women in matching tiaras, each gripping tall, curved cocktail boots. They smell like bananas. I leave. At the exit I step into the night and fill my lungs with cool desert air. The outside world rushes in to comfort me. I breathe more. The valet greets me, like a pastor after the service to shake hands with my folded-up tip. “Have a good morning, sir,” he says, even out there with the starlit envelope of night still spread above us. Car keys in hand, I breathe some more until I can see he’s right. This too is a new day. —Tom Chiarella The 10 Best Presidents, Ranked New Memos That Show Stephen Miller’s Ghoulishness My Weekend Getaway with the Proud Boys Peter Thiel’s Creepy Club for Rich Crazies The 25 Most American Movies of All Time Wait, Tulsi Gabbard Was a Weird Religious Puppet? Holy Hell, Did We Need This World Cup or What? Title: National Housing Bank appoints four industry leaders to executive team
URL: https://www.gov.uk/government/news/national-housing-bank-appoints-four-industry-leaders-to-executive-team
Time Published: 2026-06-23T09:01:06Z
Full Content:

Departments, agencies and public bodies News stories, speeches, letters and notices Detailed guidance, regulations and rules Reports, analysis and official statistics Consultations and strategy Data, Freedom of Information releases and corporate reports Quartet of highly experienced leaders join bank on a mission to accelerate housing delivery though purpose-led finance Steve Willingham, Jennifer Murray, Barry Fowler, Conan McKenzie Four senior leaders with extensive investment and real estate expertise are to join the National Housing Bank, a Homes England company. They have been appointed to executive roles at the newly launched Public Finance Institution, which is focused on accelerating the delivery of new homes and communities across England through crowding in over £50 billion of private capital to deliver more than 500,000 homes. The bank, which launched in April 2026, has confirmed the following appointments and posts following a rigorous interview process: Currently Head of Global Investment Partnerships – Debt at DRC Savills Investment Management, Steve has more than 35 years of experience in real estate finance, including as Global Head of Real Estate Finance for HSBC in London and Hong Kong and as a Managing Director at Blackrock and Merrill Lynch. He brings deep expertise across debt, structured finance and global capital markets. Steve said: I am delighted to be joining the National Housing Bank as part of the senior leadership team tasked with accelerating housing delivery across England, building on the groundwork and momentum that has been created already. This is a unique opportunity to make a difference in a nationally important field. Jennifer is currently Director of Products, Equity and Partnerships for Homes England and the National Housing Bank. She has more than 25 years of experience in real estate investment, fund management, and institutional partnerships. Since joining Homes England in 2018, Jennifer has held a number of senior roles, including Assistant Director, Partnerships & Equity, and Head of Institutional Investment. Prior to joining Homes England, she held various senior property fund management roles at Tesco Pension Investment and Schroders. Jennifer said: I originally joined Homes England because I believe firmly in the virtuous circle that pension fund and insurance investment in housing can create. Along the way we have built both a team and an investment platform that aligns financial innovation with purpose, delivering outcomes that benefit investors, residents, and society alike. It’s a privilege to continue that mission for the National Housing Bank. Bringing significant experience in real estate finance and investment, Barry has held senior leadership roles at Aviva Investors, including Director, Insurance & Investment Solutions and Managing Director, Alternative Income, Real Assets, where he was responsible for developing and delivering investment strategies across real asset classes. Prior to Aviva he was Head of Corporate Real Estate at Lloyds Banking Group. Barry said: This is a rare opportunity to apply my experience in structured investment leadership to an institution with the scale and ambition to genuinely shift outcomes in the housing market. Currently a Managing Director and portfolio manager at BlackRock, Conan has extensive experience in multi-asset investing and portfolio construction. He specialises in managing diversified strategies which blend multiple asset classes to achieve target outcomes. He has also been involved in illiquid and alternative investments for more than a decade, leading a number of large investments into infrastructure and real estate. He has been with BlackRock since 2011, initially joining as a research analyst within the Multi-Asset Strategies division. Conan said: We have an incredible opportunity to use our large capital base to make a significant positive impact on housing delivery, along with a positive financial return for the taxpayer. A decent, stable place to call home is the foundation that lets people flourish, and it’s a privilege to use the tools of investment to deliver that for more and more people. Powered by Homes England expertise, the bank aims to support the delivery of more than 500,000 homes and a raft of major regeneration and mixed-use schemes, alongside unlocking more than £53 billion of private investment over the next ten years. I am delighted we have secured the formidable skills, knowledge and experience that Steve, Jennifer, Barry and Conan bring as leaders in their own right – and excited to come together as a collective to ensure the bank, together with Homes England colleagues, builds on early successes to achieve its aims at pace and with maximum effect. Like all colleagues working for this new institution and the broader agency, we are united by a strong, purpose-driven mission to accelerate the delivery of new homes and regenerated places people want and need. These appointments mean we are in a strong position to achieve that and more, and I am looking forward to working with them as part of an outstanding team. I am pleased to see four highly talented, experienced individuals coming together to become key leaders within the bank, which has already hit the ground running with a clear, mission-driven focus that has yielded positive, early results. The board will continue to provide support, strong governance and guidance to Simon and his team as it takes full shape to ensure the bank continues to deliver effectively and at pace for the good of places and communities across the country. The new appointees are expected to take up their roles by the end of September. ENDS The National Housing Bank, a Homes England company, is a government-backed public finance institution providing purpose-led finance to accelerate housing delivery and regeneration across England. Over the next decade, it will invest up to £16 billion through debt, equity, and guarantee products, stepping in where the market cannot, and aims to attract more than £50bn of private capital to enable more homes, stronger places and thriving, sustainable communities. The bank offers partners tailored and blended finance solutions alongside the full range of Homes England support. Its delegations and flexible approach means it can respond with agility to shifting market conditions, ensuring investment reaches the areas of greatest need. Learn more: National Housing Bank - GOV.UK Homes England, the government’s housing and regeneration agency, exists to drive the creation of more affordable, quality homes and thriving places so that everyone has a place to live and grow. It makes this happen by working in partnership with thousands of organisations of all sizes, using its powers, expertise, land, capital and influence to bring investment to communities and get more quality homes built. Learn more: https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/homes-england/about Steve has more than 30 years of experience in real estate finance, with deep expertise across debt, structured finance and global capital markets. He is currently Head of Global Investment Partnerships – Debt at DRC Savills Investment Management. Prior to this, Steve spent more than a decade at HSBC, where he held several senior leadership roles in London and Hong Kong including Global Head of Real Estate Finance and Global Co-Head of Event Financing across infrastructure and real estate. His earlier experience includes senior positions at BlackRock, MGPA and Merrill Lynch, as well as a long tenure at Nationwide Building Society, where he led Structured Property Finance. Steve holds an MBA from Henley Management College. Currently Director of Products, Equity and Partnerships for the National Housing Bank and Homes England, Jennifer Murray has over 25 years of experience in real estate investment, fund management, and institutional partnerships. Since joining Homes England in 2018, Jennifer has held a number of senior roles, including Assistant Director, Partnerships & Equity, and Head of Institutional Investment, overseeing investment programmes and building strategic partnerships across the housing sector. Earlier in her career, Jennifer was Property Fund Manager at Tesco Pension Investment, and spent 16 years at Schroders, where she held roles including Fund Manager, Property Multi-Manager, Fund Manager of the Schroder Emerging Retail Fund, and Retail Asset Manager for the Schroder Exempt Property Unit Trust. She began her career as a Surveyor at Weatherall Green & Smith, developing expertise in property valuation and advisory. Barry has very significant experience in real estate finance and investment, with a strong focus on alternative income and institutional capital. Most recently, he held a number of senior leadership roles at Aviva Investors, including Director, Insurance & Investment Solutions and Managing Director, Alternative Income, Real Assets, where he was responsible for developing and delivering investment strategies across real asset classes. During his time at Aviva Investors, Barry also held governance roles including Director of the Supervisory Board of its Luxembourg business and Board Director with CEO (SMF1) responsibilities for its UK fund services entity. In addition, Barry served as a Board Director and Past Chair of CREFC Europe, contributing to the development of the European real estate finance market. Earlier in his career, he was Head of Corporate Real Estate at Lloyds Banking Group, where he led real estate lending and client coverage activities. Conan is a Managing Director at BlackRock, with extensive experience in multi-asset investing and portfolio construction. He specialises in managing highly diversified funds and mandates , with a particular emphasis on ESG integration and alternative investments, and has made a number of investments into residential housing. He has been with BlackRock since 2011, initially joining as a research analyst within the Multi-Asset Strategies division. Over this time, he has been responsible for managing or co-managing more than £10bn of multi-asset portfolios on behalf of pension schemes, charities and retail investors around the world. In particular he has led BlackRock’s successful suite of multi-asset charity funds for twelve years, and grew a multi-asset ESG fund to over 6bn euros through consistent first quartile investment performance. In addition, he leads his team’s research coverage of alternative investments, with a particular focus on illiquid asset classes. He has made significant and successful investments into UK infrastructure and real estate over the last twelve years. Title: The founder of China's Amazon wants his 700,000 blue-collar workers to be 'white-collarized' in time for AI
URL: https://www.businessinsider.com/jd-com-founder-richard-liu-ai-blue-collar-workers-robots-2026-6#article
Time Published: 2026-06-23T08:41:34Z
Full Content:
The founder of China's Amazon equivalent said his 700,000 blue-collar delivery workers will become office workers in the years to come. Richard Liu, the billionaire founder of China's e-commerce behemoth JD.com, said the company has several "Nirvana Plans" underway to "white-collarize" its 700,000 blue-collar workers, including delivery riders. Speaking at the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation CEO forum on Sunday, Liu said that in the age of AI, workers won't be needed for deliveries. Still, they will be needed to repair or troubleshoot the robots doing the manual labor. So instead of working in the field, the workers will be sitting in offices, and won't have to work nearly as hard. "JD.com has therefore signed contracts with 120 schools across China to help these blue-collar workers retrain for new roles, including repairing and maintaining robots," Liu said. Liu added that he was advocating for an internationally recognized protocol for adopting AI and robots as social systems change. He said robots should not deprive people of the right to work. The executive also said in May that JD.com would not fire any employee whose job was being replaced by robots; instead, they would be retrained and reassigned elsewhere. Deliveries via robots are already commonplace in China, including drones used to deliver food packages. For instance, food delivery giant Meituan said in 2024 that its drones could be deployed to deliver packages to hikers on the Great Wall of China. Take a smarter break in your day - and see how far you get. Liu's comments come as e-commerce giants around the world are ramping up their automation efforts to boost productivity. Amazon has a fleet of more than 750,000 robots working in its fulfillment centers. Other companies have rolled out fleets of delivery robots on city streets. DoorDash launched bright red Dot robots in Phoenix last September, which can travel on footpaths and carry up to 30 pounds of cargo. Companies like Robot.com and Starship are targeting deliveries on college campuses. Beyond gig work, automation and AI are the biggest buzzwords tied to corporate layoffs this year. Companies like Snap, Wix, and Cisco cited AI as a factor in their recent retrenchments. Jump to Every time publishes a story, you’ll get an alert straight to your inbox! Title: Lazada cuts 5% of South-east Asia staff, including in S'pore
URL: https://www.straitstimes.com/business/lazada-cuts-5-of-south-east-asia-workforce-including-roles-in-singapore
Time Published: 2026-06-23T06:13:22Z
Full Content:
Choose edition Search singapore asia world opinion life business sport Visual Podcasts STClassifieds Paid press releases Advertise with us FAQs Contact us Sign up now: Get ST's newsletters delivered to your inbox A Lazada spokesperson said on June 23 that the regional e-commerce company is “reviewing selected roles across South-east Asia”. ST PHOTO: SHINTARO TAY Sharon Salim Published Jun 23, 2026, 02:13 PM Updated Jun 23, 2026, 08:36 PM SINGAPORE - Regional e-commerce company Lazada has retrenched 5 per cent of its workforce in South-east Asia, including an undisclosed number of employees in Singapore, according to a source familiar with the matter. When contacted, a Lazada spokesperson said on June 23 that the company is “reviewing selected roles across South-east Asia”. “Where roles are affected, employees will be engaged directly and with care, respect and dignity,” the spokesperson told The Straits Times. “We are committed to supporting affected employees through the transition with relevant guidance and support, in line with applicable requirements.” This comes after Lazada’s e-commerce rival Shopee axed hundreds of developer jobs globally earlier in June. In early May, Amazon also announced job cuts in Singapore as the e-commerce giant shifted its focus from local grocery fulfilment to international product shipments. Derrick Teo, chief executive of manpower consultancy firm Elitez Group, said the e-commerce industry is still growing, but the period of rapid hiring, heavy subsidies and growth at all costs has ended. “Platforms are expected to process more transactions while controlling costs and improving profitability. Technology and artificial intelligence are helping companies operate more efficiently, meaning business growth may no longer produce the same level of headcount growth seen during the (Covid-19) pandemic,” he said. Online chatter surfaced about two weeks ago when a Reddit user commented about Lazada’s impending job cuts in a forum that discusses recent layoffs and workplace culture in Singapore. ST understands that affected Lazada employees were notified on June 23, with its human resources team engaging them directly. Its staff in Singapore are based in the Lazada One office in Bras Basah Road. Lazada said it is working closely with the Food, Drinks and Allied Workers Union (FDAWU) to ensure support is rendered for affected employees and for the process to be “handled responsibly”. FDAWU general-secretary Sankaradass S. Chami said Lazada had informed the union in advance about the job cuts. “This advance notification reflects the positive labour-management relationship and trust built over time,” he said, adding that Lazada will provide one year of union membership and training grant support for affected union members. Lazada previously laid off an undisclosed number of staff in January 2024 without consulting the FDAWU, under which it is unionised. The union had expressed its disappointment then, and the matter was escalated to the Ministry of Manpower (MOM). The Taskforce for Responsible Retrenchment and Employment Facilitation also noted that Lazada had notified the task force about its restructuring exercise, which is part of a larger organisational realignment. “Lazada is committed to providing a retrenchment package aligned with the Tripartite Advisory on Managing Excess Manpower and Responsible Retrenchment,” said a spokesperson for the task force, which comprises MOM, Workforce Singapore, National Trades Union Congress and NTUC’s e2i (Employment and Employability Institute). In the 2024 job cuts, the FDAWU negotiated improved retrenchment benefits for the laid-off Lazada employees, who had initially been offered two weeks’ salary for each year of service – a figure below industry norms. MOM facilitated the discussions between the union and Lazada. Affected employees were notified months later that some would receive an extra two weeks’ pay per year of service, while others would get training support of $1,200. A round of layoffs had also been conducted in October 2023. Lazada’s 2024 job cuts turned the spotlight on the company, increasing speculation about a potential initial public offering for its parent business unit that year. It came amid growing competition in South-east Asia’s e-commerce landscape. Postings on professional networking platform LinkedIn suggest that Lazada has about 760 employees in Singapore. A June 23 check by ST on job openings on the platform shows at least 10 roles in Bangkok, Manila and the Philippines. A job opening for Singapore was posted a month ago. On the Lazada website, however, no job openings can be found. Founded in 2012, Lazada, which became a subsidiary of Chinese tech giant Alibaba Group Holding in 2016, has a presence in Singapore, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Thailand and Vietnam. While Lazada remains the second-largest e-commerce player in Singapore, just behind Shopee, its estimated market share in South-east Asia fell in 2025 as its rivals grew faster, said Teo. “This stronger competition may have pushed Lazada to accelerate its restructuring and operate with a leaner workforce, which contributed to the retrenchment exercise. However, this remains an industry-based interpretation as Lazada officially attributed the cuts to business needs and organisational efficiency,” he added. Lazada recently marked its 14th anniversary with a birthday sale, its first major regional campaign for 2026. The platform’s use of AI features has become more apparent as they are integrated into product pages. Additional reporting by Vihanya Rakshika and Emerald Lo Sharon Salim is a business correspondent at The Straits Times, with a focus on jobs, workplace culture and trends. Title: Bouygues Telecom Enhances Quality-of-Experience for Millions of French Broadband Subscribers with AI-Driven Expresse from AXON Networks
URL: https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/bouygues-telecom-enhances-quality-of-experience-for-millions-of-french-broadband-subscribers-with-ai-driven-expresse-from-axon-networks-302806762.html
Time Published: 2026-06-23T06:00:00Z
Full Content:
Searching for your content... In-Language News Contact Us 888-776-0942 from 8 AM - 10 PM ET Jun 23, 2026, 02:00 ET Share this article Leading French service provider upgrades broadband service assurance capabilities with advanced AI solution delivering enhanced network visibility and a superior customer experience MEUDON LA FORÊT, France and IRVINE, Calif., June 23, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Bouygues Telecom, a leading France-based provider of mobile, internet, and IPTV services, and AXON Networks, a global leader in intelligent autonomous network and connectivity assurance platforms, today announced that Bouygues Telecom is expanding their long-term deployment of the Expresse Access Network Assurance solution, part of the AXON Maestro platform, to support both DSL and PON environments. As part of this evolution, Bouygues Telecom is replacing its legacy SNMP based monitoring service assurance platform with the Expresse solution, featuring an architecture focused on AI analysis, telemetry and modern GUI for efficient customer support. This strategic shift will enable significantly deeper, real-time visibility across complex, multi-vendor network environments. By leveraging the AI-driven analytics and streaming telemetry of Expresse, Bouygues Telecom will enhance the quality-of-experience for approximately six million fixed broadband subscribers nationwide, while improving the management of field operations and accelerating issue resolution as Bouygues moves toward autonomous networking. The deployment reflects a broader transition toward next-generation service assurance, replacing legacy tools with AXON Network's AI-native, more scalable and future-ready approach that leverages deep domain expertise built from the over 97 million subscriber lines globally currently leveraging their solutions. "As our network evolves toward greater scale and complexity, it has been essential to move beyond the limitations of traditional SNMP-based monitoring," said Jean-Paul Arzel, Chief Technical Officer of Bouygues Telecom. "Expresse from AXON Networks will provide the depth of visibility and multi-vendor telemetry capabilities we were looking for, enabling a more proactive and automated approach to service assurance. This path of evolution will play a key role in improving both operational efficiency and customer experience." "Service providers are increasingly recognizing the need to modernize legacy assurance environments that were not designed for today's fiber-centric, multi-vendor networks," said Martin Manniche, CEO and founder of AXON Networks. "Bouygues Telecom's expansion of Expresse reflects a broader industry shift toward real-time telemetry and AI-driven operations that optimize performance, autonomous networks, and connectivity experience. We are proud to support them in this transformation." About Expresse Expresse features a sophisticated diagnostics engine leveraging artificial intelligence, machine learning, and modern streaming telemetry, all hallmarks of the AXON Maestro platform, to transform how network performance is monitored and managed. Unlike traditional SNMP-based systems—which provide limited, periodic snapshots of network health—Expresse harnesses real-time, high-frequency telemetry data across multi-vendor environments, delivering granular and continuous visibility into network and service performance. Key capabilities include: Expresse and its AI-driven analytics combine industry-leading telemetry with over a decade of domain expertise drawn from the network and service experience of many of the world's largest broadband operators to provide unrivalled insights into network optimization and performance improvement. Service providers using Expresse have reported: About Bouygues Telecom A subsidiary of the Bouygues group, Bouygues Telecom is a global French telecommunications and digital services operator. In 2025, the company achieved a turnover of 8.1 billion euros, employs 10,700 people, and has 501 stores in France. Founded in 1994, Bouygues Telecom is committed to providing its individual customers, businesses, and public administrations with secure, innovative, and high-quality Fixed and Mobile communication services, as well as very high-speed internet services, by constantly developing its network and user experience. 27.1 million Mobile customers and 5.4 million Fixed customers trust Bouygues Telecom, the number 1 operator for WiFi and mobile internet connections according to nPerf in 2025, and number 1 for Mobile in dense areas according to ARCEP, in 2025. Its 4G network now covers 99% of the population, and its 5G network covers more than 19,900 municipalities and over 86% of the population. Bouygues Telecom's Enterprise division supports and helps grow a community of over 120,000 clients, including four out of five major CAC 40 accounts, by generalizing Very High-Speed Broadband in France and facilitating the adoption of new uses such as unified communications, smart networks, and corporate mobility services, and by supporting the transformation of their digital infrastructures. Committed to reducing its carbon emissions, Bouygues Telecom aims to achieve -29.4% for scopes 1 and 2 and -17.5% for scope 3 by 2027, objectives approved by the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi). #OnEstFaitPourEtreEnsemble To follow Bouygues Telecom news: corporate.bouyguestelecom.fr, on X: @ByTel_Corporate About AXON Networks AXON Networks is a global leader in intelligent autonomous network and connectivity assurance platforms, known for its award-winning Digital Twin technology that enables real-time, predictive, and self-optimizing operations and extraordinary connectivity experiences. Its AXON Maestro platform and solutions are used by service providers and enterprises worldwide to unlock the full potential of advanced networks, transforming them into agile AI connectivity enablers with hyperscaler-like network operations. AXON Network's platforms are relied upon by over 30 leading operators worldwide to serve a rapidly increasing base of over 97 million managed users and provide unrivalled AN-4 focused network autonomy, orchestration, security, and insights to bring the best AI connected experiences to everyone, everywhere.
